title: Ex Vivo Models to Study the Interaction of Immune Cells and Cancer Cells under Therapy
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"dbx1y","text":"Recent developments in cancer therapeutics have focused on modulating the immune component of the tumor microenvironment in order to activate anti-tumoral effector functions and induce cytotoxic immune responses. To further advance the design of treatment strategies, it is essential to understand what factors within the tumor microenvironment limit therapeutic efficacy and immune responses. Intravital imaging is a pioneering technology that has been utilized to study these processes in an intact environment(1), successfully highlighting the importance of cancer cell-immune cell interactions such as cancer cell killing caused by longitudinal and repeated interactions with immune cells(2). Despite the clear advantages that intravital imaging provides, its low throughput limits the variety of drugs, genetically altered parameters, and cell-cell interactions that can be studied(3-5). In other fields, a complementary approach has been to turn to ex vivo models which offer greater throughput and simpler experimental protocols(6-8). Here, we describe the setup of an ex vivo technique to study dynamic interactions within an intact environment. This chapter will, in detail, 1) demonstrate strategies to generate fluorescently tagged cancer and immune cells using genetic as well as dye-based approaches; 2) present a method for how to best generate lung metastasis for ex vivo imaging; and 3) describe the protocol to generate precision cut lung metastasis slices; and 4) detail how to prepare them for intravital imaging. This protocol can be utilized for following the effect of therapeutics on tumor growth within the lung microenvironment ex vivo, and on the interaction of cancer cells with immune cells. Ex vivo lung slices are an excellent model to 1) prepare the design of follow on intravital imaging experiments (e.g. determine appropriate fluorophores, drug concentrations, or cell types to label), 2) study immune and cancer cell interactions in a complete environment, 3) select promising drugs from a pool of targets, and 4) select the best timing for intravital imaging experiments.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"eq1vg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"wscpd","text":"One in six lives are cut short by cancer every year, making it the second leading cause of death worldwide(9). Metastasis accounts for the majority of cancer-related deaths(10). In breast cancer patients, the vast majority of patients succumb to metastasis that predominantly occur in the bone, brain, and lung(11). In recent years, new therapies focused on harnessing the tumor microenvironment and the immune system have been developed with the goal of fully eradicating cancer cells and benefiting patient outcomes(12). Unfortunately, to date, these therapies have only shown limited success rates. This is likely due to i) mutations and mechanisms which prevent the penetration of cytotoxic immune cells into tumors(13, 14), ii) inhibitory signals which dampen anti-tumor immune responses(15, 16), and iii) unappreciated differences in cellular environments between primary and secondary sites(17, 18). Therefore, much attention is shifting towards understanding the limitations of these therapies and studying the dynamics of therapy responses.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"owtoi","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"coyhj","text":"Intravital imaging of cancer has provided essential insight into the diverse steps of metastasis such as intravasation(19, 20), extravasation(21-23) and seeding(21) and outgrowth(24) of metastatic cells. These studies have shown that under chronic lung inflammation, neutrophils release extracellular traps that can modulate the microenvironment to awaken the dormant cancer cells(24); that macrophages can facilitate the dissemination of cancer cells by modulating vascular opening at TMEM doorways, allowing motile cancer cells to spread hematogenously(19); and that metastatic dissemination can sometimes be enhanced by neoadjuvant chemotherapy(25). These discoveries about the dynamics of tumor progression and metastasis could not have been made using fixed tissue analyses alone.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"pwcxd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"lsl9n","text":"However, the setup of an intravital imaging experiment requires the establishment and optimization of many parameters, including i) choosing well-designed and appropriate animal models, ii) optimizing concentrations for drug treatments and cell injections, iii) determining the optimal fluorophores and dyes for labeling cells and processes of interest, iv) timeline for optimal insertion of longitudinal lung window. All of this must be accomplished while using a refined surgical and imaging setup, and employing techniques and equipment to maintain the vitality of the animals throughout the experiments, leading to a low throughput, time intensive, and costly process. One solution to this difficulty, is to be able to turn to a model which replicates the natural in vivo context that cancer cells exist within and allows multiple experimental conditions to be determined using a single mouse, while eliminating the need for surgical protocols or for monitoring the health and vitality of the mouse. These conditions can all be satisfied using the precision cut lung slice model (PCLS).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ad9l3","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rfxdw","text":"The precision cut lung slice model has been well-established in the field of respiratory infectious diseases and has been used to study changes in the lung matrix induced upon infection(7, 8). These studies included the influence and signaling pathways of profibrotic growth factors on the induction of early-fibrotic changes(26); cytokine responses to bacterial stimuli and viral infection(27); and changes in mesenchymal, epithelial, endothelial, and immune cell types upon fibrotic stimulus(28).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"wihmt","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ci9hr","text":"In our laboratory, we have combined the PCLS protocol with breast cancer metastasis models to investigate dynamic cancer cell interactions within the lung environment upon therapy (Figure 1). In particular, we have focused on understanding the dynamics of immune cell-cancer cell interactions during therapeutic treatment. Recent research has shown that cancer cells only can be eliminated by cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) through multiple hits that occur with a frequency of less than 1 hit in 50min. CTL-tumor cell interactions that occur at slower rates allow the cancer cells to initiate repair processes and avoid killing(2). Thus, it is believed that the most effective therapies are those that can induce a high density of intratumoral CTLs to increase the odds of rapid serial CTL-cancer cell interactions. Indeed, the recent intravital imaging study by Lau et al. showed that successful therapy, including vaccinations or immunotherapy, alters immune cell velocity and adherence to targets(29) with significant decreases in cell velocity and prolonged dwell times at targets resulting in a higher probability of tumor cell killing.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":181,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"qdrac","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"csmnl","text":"Here, we describe a model combining precision cut lung slices (PCLS) with breast cancer metastasis models to aid in the optimization of intravital imaging experiments and to study essential interactions of tumor and immune cells upon therapy. This model enables the high-throughput study of the dynamic changes in tumor and immune cells upon therapy in an intact environment and can provide fundamental information for the design of further intravital imaging studies such as selecting i) the most favorable drug combinations, ii) the optimal timing for intravital imaging studies and iii) the best combination of fluorophores for intravital imaging.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fczt4","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Multiple methods can be applied to generate metastasis in the lung. These methods have been reviewed elsewhere(30). Methods can vary from genetically engineered mouse models of metastasis, to fat pad injected cell lines that spontaneously develop metastasis, to experimental metastasis (intra-venous injected cell lines). The time for development of metastasis depends heavily on cell line, route of administration, injected cell number, and background of mice/cell lines and must be determined empirically for each model. The method presented here covers both the spontaneous (orthotopic injection followed by resection) and experimental metastasis (tail vein injection) methods (20) using MMTV-PyMT derived cells containing the fluorescent protein Cerulean (Cer2-MMTV PyMT).

Culture Cer2-MMTV PyMT cells with complete DMEM in a T75cm² flask with filter cap to between 30-70% confluency. Passage cells on the day prior to injection to reach about 70% confluency on the day of injection (see Note 3).
To detach Cer2-MMTV PyMT cells flask, remove complete DMEM and rinse flask once with PBS to remove remaining protein and cell media.

Add 2mL of Trypsin-EDTA per flask and ensure that liquid is covering cells. Incubate cells for about 10 min at 37C, 5% CO₂ in incubator to detach cells. Observe detachment of cells under a widefield microscope.
Neutralize Trypsin-EDTA with 10mL complete DMEM and transfer solution into 50 mL Falcon tube.
Centrifuge cells for 5 min at 300g, room temperature.
Discard supernatant and vigorously resuspend cells in 1 mL ice-cold PBS, top up to 10 mL with ice-cold PBS and repeat centrifugation.
Resuspend cell pellet in 1 mL ice-cold PBS and pass through 30µm cell strainer to remove and dissociate cell clusters.
Count cells using hemocytometer or cell counter (CellDrop FL, Denovix).

Adjust cell density to 5x10⁶ cells per mL in PBS for tail vein injections (1x10⁶ cells per mouse) and 5x10⁶ in 1:1 PBS:Matrigel (2.5x10⁵ cells per mouse) for fat pad injection. Ensure that for both injections cell suspension is kept continuously on ice.
The day prior to injection, shave mice from fourth and fifth nipple to midline and weigh mice to monitor weight changes after injection.
Autoclave a pair of tweezers and place an aliquot of matrigel on ice at 4°C to thaw overnight.
On the day of injection, anesthetize the mice with 5% isoflurane for 1 min, then decrease dose of isoflurane to 2%.
Confirm anesthesia by toe pinch and wait until there is no reflex observed.
Apply ophthalmic ointment to eyes of mice to prevent drying.
Clean shaved area with a chlorhexidine soaked cotton swab using circular movement away from the incision site.
Using the tweezers, gently lift tissue at fifth nipple and insert the needle bezel up, gently moving in a diagonal line to the fourth nipple, while keeping the needle close to skin.
While holding the needle horizontally, inject 50µL of cell suspension into the mammary fat pad around fourth nipple. A weal should be observed where the cell suspension was injected.
Slowly withdraw needle to avoid leakage from injection site.
Return mice to a heated cage (using a heat lamp or heat pad). Monitor mice for 15 minutes after they regain consciousness.
Administer 0.1 µg/mL antibiotic (baytril) into the drinking water to minimize the risk of infection.
Resect primary tumor when it reaches 5mmx5mm and macro-metastases can be observed: around four months after orthotopic injections.
Pre-heat water bath to 37°C.
Prepare 200µL cell suspensions per 29G ½ inch syringe per mouse: remove air bubbles and position on ice until use.
Position mouse in a restrainer to avoid sudden movements of the mouse.
To ensure dilation of veins, warm the mouse tail by dipping the tail into the water bath (about 1-2min, avoid restraining the mice for an extended duration to minimize stress).
If mouse has turned within the restrainer, confirm the orientation and location of lateral vein before carrying out injections.

Insert the needle with bevel up into the vein towards the direction of the head and keep the syringe parallel to the tail. There is no need for deep insertion of needle into the tail vein as the veins are superficially located. Proper placement can be verified when there is no resistance to injection and the needle advances slowly into the vein. (see Note 4)
Slowly inject the cell suspension into the vein, if the needle is inserted correctly, then the vein will appear to blanch during the injection.
Remove the needle and apply gentle compression to the tail to prevent bleeding.
Return mouse to cage and monitor for 15min to observe any adverse effects.
Macrometastases can be observed around 4 weeks after tail vein injection.
Sterilize forceps, scissors, and disposable razor blades.
Cool PBS and complete DMEM (4°C)
Pre-cool metallic buffer holder and disposable blades for 1h at -20°C prior to slicing.
Prepare 24-well plate by pipetting 1 mL ice-cold complete DMEM into each well and transfer plate on ice until slicing.
Prepare two 50mL aliquots of complete DMEM and PBS and store at 4°C for after lung dissection.
Pre-heat 2% LMP Agarose and transfer at 37°C until lung inflation (see Note 5)
Humanely euthanize C57BL/6 mouse with a fatal dose of ketamine (see Note 6).
Sterilize mouse chest and tracheal area using 70% Ethanol spray.
Make a lateral incision from the lower end of the rib cage to the upper end of the throat.
Open chest cavity along the midline from abdomen to chin and remove front ribs to generate sufficient space for lung inflation.
Cut artery between lungs and liver for exsanguination and to confirm death of mice.
Perfuse lungs through the left/right cardiac ventricle with 10mL ice cold PBS using 10mL syringe fitted with 25G needle.
Expose trachea by carefully removing surrounding muscle tissue.
Gently lift trachea with forceps and make a loose knot around it.
Laterally insert 19-21G needle into trachea using forceps to help guide the needle and secure needle by tightening the knot.
Fill 2mL syringe with 2% pre-heated LMP Agarose and connect to 19-21G syringe.
Inflate lungs with about 1 mL LMP Agarose (see Notes 7 & 8). Optimal inflation of lungs can be observed when tips of lung lobes stiffen. Ensure that lung lobes are not over-inflated (see Note 9).
Leave needle and syringe attached until solidification of agarose (2-3min).
Cover lung with sterile parafilm and cover with ice-cold PBS or ice cubes to facilitate solidification.
Remove needle and tighten knot, cut trachea above knot, and, holding trachea, excise lung and move to 10mL dish with ice-cold PBS.
Place dish on ice until further processing.
Turn on hood around vibratome and spray area and blade holder with 70% Ethanol. Remove metallic buffer holder from the freezer and sterilize with 70% Ethanol, connect to vibratome.
Take a disposable blade from –20°C and screw into blade holder.
Transfer lung slices into a fresh 10cm dish with forceps and use dissection scissors to separate individual lobes.
Glue one lung lobe into the middle of the specimen holder with cyanoacarylate glue and place into buffer holder.
Fill buffer holder with PBS/1%BSA until lung lobe is immersed in buffer.
Lower blade to about 1mm above specimen and set parameters for cutting (thickness: 300µm, cutting speed: 7, cutting oscillation: 5).
Use single slice mode and select start position for the blade about 2mm behind the tissue and select the end position about 2mm from back of the tissue.
Switch to continuous slicing mode and proceed with slicing of the tissue.
Use a plastic loop to gently transfer lung slices into a 24 well plate prepared on ice.
To cut additional lobes, remove buffer using 50mL pipette and dry before addition of new specimen.
Repeat step 25-31.
Incubate lung lobes at 37°C, 5% CO₂ overnight before preparation of imaging.

Novel development of therapies for metastatic cancer focus on modulating the environment and immune responses at the metastatic site to increase susceptibility of cancer cells to treatment. To assess whether therapies change the microenvironment, dynamic changes between immune cells and cancer cells and the behavior of cells upon treatment have to be examined. In order to evaluate these interactions ex vivo and in real-time, other cells of interest have to be labelled using fluorescent tags (see Note 10). Celltracker dye forms succinimdyl esters with free amine groups of cellular proteins.
To label cells using commercially available Celltrackers, centrifuge cells of interest (Note 11) at 300g, 5 min, room temperature, discard supernatant and resuspend in 1 mL PBS.
Count cells using hemocytometer or cell counter (CellDrop FL, Denovix).
Aliquot number of cells needed, centrifuge at 300g, 5 min, room temperature and resuspend at 1x10⁶ cells per mL in PBS.
Add 0.5 µM Celltracker™ Green CMFDA and incubate for 25min on the rotary shaker at room temperature.
Centrifuge cells at 300g, 5 min, room temperature and resuspend in complete phenol red free media at 10⁶ cells per mL.
Preheat complete Phenol-red free DMEM and 2% LMP Agarose at 37°C in the water bath
Autoclave custom-made stainless-steel window

Transfer one lung slice per well into 24 well glass bottom plate and secure lung tissue with custom-made stainless-steel window frame (allowing media to access the lung tissue while securing the tissue edges for imaging).
Put four drops of LMP Agarose around stainless-steel window to avoid movement of window and allow micro-cartography(31).
If investigating immune cell and cancer cell interactions, add 100µL of immune cells on top of metastatic lung slices.
Incubate for 15 min at 37°C to support migration of cells into the tissue
Add 900µL of phenol-red free complete DMEM as well as additional treatments of interest (see Note 12).
Plates can be imaged immediately or incubated at 37°C, 5% CO₂ until imaging experiment.
Turn on heater for imaging stage at 37°C and, for longitudinal live imaging, use an imaging stage with built-in incubator with 37°C and 5% CO2 (see Note 13).
Turn on epifluorescent lamp and lasers on the multiphoton or confocal microscope.
Change microscope stage insert to one that accepts a multiwell plate and position plate on microscope within incubator.

For imaging experiments, use of a 25x1.05 NA water immersion lens is preferable. Add one drop of MilliQ water on top of lens and focus on lung tissue using epifluorescence light, observing the autofluorescence of tissue (green or blue) or the fluorescent cell labels (Note 14).

Microcartography: To follow the effect of a therapy over multiple days, microcartography can be used to repeated relocalize multiple fields of view over many imaging sessions (Figure 2). This is accomplished by recording and comparing the coordinates of etched grooves in the custom-made stainless-steel window frame at each imaging session, as previously described(31).
When focused on tissue, set stage coordinates to x,y,z = 0.
Using brightfield imaging, find the etched grooves on the top left window. Record the xy location for each of the three grooves and save their xy coordinates.
Return back to the tissue and find positions of interest, recording their xy locations.
When relocalization is needed for next imaging session, repeat step 1 to 6 and use mathematic prediction software(31) to predict the new coordinates of the positions of interest.
Labeling of nucleus and apoptosis: For visualization of all cells and to measure apoptosis induction upon therapy, add 1 uM NucSpot Live 650 (nucleus) and 150 nM Apotracker™ Green (Figure 3).
Z-Stack for time lapse imaging is set by focusing about 25μm below top (closest to coverslip) of tissue in 3-5μm steps for 50μm (see Note 15).
Duration of time lapse imaging depends on kinetics of interactions as well as frequency of events.

Dynamics of immune cells and cancer cells can be studied using the ROI-Tracker plugin and associated motility parameters Excel spreadsheet calculator as published by our group in 2011(32) (Figure 4). The method for use of ROI_Tracker for ImageJ is as followed:
Open timelapse of interest and separate individual channels.
Threshold image to mark the cells of interest.
Open ROI Manager and draw a region of interest around the area the cell of interest is moving in.
Use the Analyze Particles feature and add the resultant positions to ROI Manager.
Export and save positions to a file.
Open ROI_Tracker and add positions saved in ROI Manager.
To track movement path, select animate and path.
Export parameters calculated by ROI Tracker.
Open ROI_Tracker in the analysis Excel sheet and insert calculate parameters.
Velocity, Average Speed, and Directionality are calculated automatically by the spreadsheet.

To fully understand the therapeutic responses observed through intravital imaging, lung slices can be fixed and further analyzed for markers of the microenvironment or diverse immune cells. Here we describe an example staining protocol to analyze proliferation and leukocyte numbers within the lung slices.
Wash lung slices twice in PBS supplemented with 2 mM EDTA for 10min at room temperature.
Fix slices for 2h in 1% paraformaldehyde at room temperature in the dark.
Wash sections for about one hour in PBS-T, changing PBS-T three times every 20 min at room temperature.
Remove excess PBS-T and block slices in blocking buffer for 2h at room temperature.
Aspirate blocking buffer and add primary antibodies for CD45 (1:500) and Ki67 (1:100) in blocking buffer
Cover slices and incubate overnight in a humidified environment at 4°C.
Remove primary antibody and wash three times in PBS-T for 15 min.
Dilute secondary antibodies 1:1000 in blocking buffer and incubate for 2h at room temperature in the dark.
Wash three times in PBS-T for 15min and incubate with 3ug/mL Hoechst 33342 (in PBS) for 30min at room temperature in the dark.
Image immediately on the confocal or two-photon microscope (see Figure 5).

Troubleshooting notes: Note 1: Animals should be housed in specific pathogen free conditions with 12h light-dark cycle, 45-65% humidity and 21-25°C. Note 2: MMTV-PyMT cells (provided by Dr. Mikala Egeblad’s laboratory at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory, NY) were isolated from spontaneous mammary tumors of transgenic MMTV-PyMT C57BL/6 mice and immortalized. Note 3: Cer2 MMTV PyMT cells at 70-80% confluency will give you about 4x10 6 cells per T75cm 2 flask. Note 4: Tail vein injections: Grasp tail distal (close to tip) to mid-length and position index and middle finger of non-dominant hand around tail below where the needle will be inserted. Slightly tense tail and enter needle parallel to tail. Note 5: LMP Agarose can be prepared and reheated for about a month, but do not reheat more than five times as Agarose concentration increases with every reheating step due to water evaporation. To ensure sterility, the mouse dissection must be performed in a culture hood. Note 6: Mice can be euthanized through cervical dislocation but take care not to damage the trachea to avoid tracheal dissociation and leakage of LMP agarose. Note 7: Ensure that needle is positioned with bezel upright for lung inflation. Note 8: To avoid altering tissue composition and viability, ensure that LMP Agarose is cool to touch before use; it should be around 37-39°C for use. Note 9: Lung lobes are over-inflated when lungs start to leak agarose after inflation of lobes. If Agarose is leaking from the beginning, then the needle either punctured the trachea, or has to be readjusted. Note 10: Using fluorescent labeling of cells with Celltracker dyes is sufficient for tracking of cells for up to eight divisions, as fluorescent signal gradually decreases with each division. If it is of interest to monitor cells for extended time periods, then other labeling techniques might be considered. The simplest alternative would be to stain co-cultures at every imaging session with fluorescently tagged antibodies that target cells of interest(33, 34). Alternatively, lentiviral transduction of cells with fluorescent labels might be considered (35). Although this is a very stable technique for incorporating fluoroscent protein expression into the cells, some naïve immune cells such as NK cells or lymphocytes only have shown limited success rates for permanent transductions and transfection. Finally, cells may be isolated from genetically engineered mice that express fluorescent proteins, either in all cells or in particular cells of interest. Note 11: For this method, cells were isolated from the spleen of mice with metastatic breast cancer using mouse CD8 T cell isolation Kit from Miltenyi Biotec according to manufacturer’s instructions. Magnet cell sorting provides a quick an easy method to isolate cells with high purity. Cell purity can be verified by fluorescence activated cell scanning, in this case with AF488-CD3 and AF647-CD8. Other methods might include percoll/ficoll gradients or fluorescence activated cell sorting. Negative and positive isolation kits for multiple immune and stromal cell populations within the tumor microenvironment can be purchased from Miltenyi Biotec (MACS) or Stemcell Technologies (EasySep). Note 12: To study CD8 T cell interactions, low concentrations of stimuli have to be added to support survival of cells, including 40IU/mL IL-2, 2 µg/mL aCD3 and 5 µg/mL aCD28. Note 13: HEPES within the culture media works as a buffer to avoid acidification. Note 14: If no metastatic cells can be found within tissue, or if there is interest in studying outgrowth of cancer cells upon seeding in pre-conditioned lungs, cancer cells can be added on top of lung slices. In this case, on the day of preparation of lung slices, seed 25x10 4 Cer2 MMTV PyMT cells on top of precision cut lung slices and allow to culture for 24h. Use these slices for imaging, and expansion of cells can be observed over time. Be aware that the biology may differ between both models as the influx of immune cells that is normally observed when metastases are present might not occur to the same extent, and response to therapy may differ ( Figure 6 ). Note 15: It is important to also note and remember the order in which the location of each groove is recorded.

If performed properly, it will be possible to visualize the interaction between immune cells and tumor cells using rapid time lapse microscopy ( Figure 4 , Supplementary Movie 1 ) over multiple days ( Figures 2 and Figure 3 ). This framework allows testing the impact of therapeutics on the dynamic behavior of cells (tumor cell and immune cell growth, motility, and interactions) over time ( Figure 4, Supplementary Movie 1 ). Additional information can be garnered by employing the use of fluorescent biosensors for functional states, such as Apo15, which can report upon the activation of apoptosis pathways ( Figure 3 ).

Figure 1. Figure 1: Schematic of preparation of metastatic lung slices and setup of imaging plate. 4 – 6 weeks after injection of Cer2 MMTV PyMT cells, lungs are harvested and inflated with 2% LMP Agarose. Using a vibratome, 300μm sections are cut and transferred into a 24 well plate. If there are no metastatic cells present in lung slices, Cer2 OVA MMTV PyMT cells can be seeded on the lung tissue for 24h prior to addition of CD8 T cells and transfer to glass bottom plate (Option b). After 24h, lung slices are transferred to a 24 well glass-bottom imaging plate and secured in place with stainless steel window. The window is secured with a surrounding droplet of 2% LMP Agarose. After transfer into imaging plate, drug treatments can be added as well as additional cell populations. This is possible as the inside of window is accessible to the media. Figure created with BioRender.com. Figure 2. Figure 2: Expansion of Cer2 MMTV PyMT cells along lung matrix. Lung slices are imaged on consecutive days after preparation of slices. Cer2 OVA PyMT cells shown in cyan. Cancer cells expand along lung matrix. Scale bar: 50 µm. Figure 3. Figure 3: Chemotherapy-induced apoptosis in Cer2 MMTV PyMT cells upon culture. Cer2 MMTV PyMT cells are treated with 0µM or 10 µM cyclophosphamide for 4- to 7- days. Representative images of Cer2 MMTV PyMT cells (cyan) and apoptosis (green). Scale bar: 50 µm. Figure 4: Dynamics of CD8 T cell movement and directed movement with Cer2 MMTV PyMT cells. A) A CMDFA+ CD8 T cell (green, purple outline and arrow) can be seen following a Cer2 MMTV PyMT cell (cyan, beige outline and arrow) over a time course of 4.5hrs. Perimeters of CD8 T cell (purple) and Cer2 MMTV PyMT (beige) have been overlaid on the movie. b) Net path displacement of the CD8 T cell (purple) and Cer2 MMTV PyMT (beige). Figure 4. A) A CMDFA+ CD8 T cell (green, purple outline and arrow) can be seen following a Cer2 MMTV PyMT cell (cyan, beige outline and arrow) over a time course of 4.5hrs. Perimeters of CD8 T cell (purple) and Cer2 MMTV PyMT (beige) have been overlaid on the movie. b) Net path displacement of the CD8 T cell (purple) and Cer2 MMTV PyMT (beige). Figure 5. Figure 5: Number of Leukocyte and proliferating cells in lung slices with and without treatment with 10 µM cyclophosphamide for seven days.E xample images of untreated (Top) and cyclophosphamide treated (bottom) slices showing leukocytes (CD45, red) and proliferation (Ki67, green) and nuclear stain (Hoechst 33342, blue). Scale bar: 50 µm. Figure 6. Figure 6: Differences in the percentage of CD45+ and Ki67+ of all cells when metastases are formed in vivo, or upon seeding ex vivo. Percentage of CD45 + (a) and ki57 + (b) cells untreated (UT) or treated with cyclophosphamide (CP) for seven days when Cer2 MMTV PyMT cells were administered in vivo, or ex vivo seeded. Quantification represents three independent experiments. One-way ANOVA with multiple comparison, p = * ≤0.05, **≤0.01. (Be aware of different levels of leukocyte responses for in vivo and ex vivo seeded Cer2 PyMT cells for experimental design).
